title: Python Programming
description: Master Python from basics to advanced concepts including OOP, data structures, and real-world projects.

subjects.title: Introduction to Python
subjects.notes:
Python is a high-level, interpreted programming language. Variables are dynamically typed. Use `print()` to output. Indentation is mandatory.

```python
# Hello World
print('Hello, CODEazy!')

# Variables
name = 'Alice'
age = 20
pi = 3.14
```

subjects.title: Data Types & Variables
subjects.notes:
Python has several built-in data types: int, float, str, bool, list, tuple, dict, set.

```python
# Numeric
x = 42          # int
y = 3.14        # float

# String
s = 'Python'

# List (mutable)
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']

# Tuple (immutable)
coords = (10, 20)

# Dictionary
student = {'name': 'Alice', 'grade': 90}
```

subjects.title: Control Flow
subjects.notes:
Control flow lets you make decisions and repeat actions.

```python
# If-elif-else
score = 85
if score >= 90:
    print('A')
elif score >= 80:
    print('B')
else:
    print('C')

# For loop
for i in range(5):
    print(i)

# While loop
count = 0
while count < 3:
    print(count)
    count += 1
```

subjects.title: Functions & Modules
subjects.notes:
Functions are reusable blocks of code. Modules let you organise and reuse code across files.

```python
# Define a function
def greet(name, greeting='Hello'):
    return f'{greeting}, {name}!'

print(greet('Alice'))
print(greet('Bob', 'Hi'))

# Lambda
square = lambda x: x ** 2

# Import a module
import math
print(math.sqrt(16))
```

subjects.title: Strings1
subjects.notes:
Python String

Strings are sequence of characters written inside quotes. It can include letters, numbers, symbols and spaces. Python does not have a separate character type.

A single character is treated as a string of length one.
Strings are commonly used for text handling and manipulation.
Strings can be created using either Single quotes ' '
Double quotes " "
Triple quotes ''' ''' or """ """ (used for multiline string

  • A single character is treated as a string of length one.

  • Strings are commonly used for text handling and manipulation.

Creating a String

Strings can be created using either Single quotes ' '

  • Double quotes " "

  • Triple quotes ''' ''' or """ """ (used for multiline string

Example:

name = "Python" city = 'Jodhpur' message = """Welcome to Python Programming"""

Characteristics of Python Strings

  • Immutable: Once a string is created, we cannot change it. Any operation in terms of modifying a string will actually make a new string.

  • Ordered: Strings are ordered collections of characters where each character has a fixed index (starting from 0). We can access the characters using their position.

(starting from 0). We can access the characters using their position.
We can iterate through each character of a string using Python loops, like for, like for
Support Slicing: We can slice a string to extract substrings with the help of [start : end] syntax.
Unicode Support: By default, strings in Python 3 are stored as Unicode. This allows us to handle
international languages and emojis efficiently.
Dynamic Length: We can initialize a string of any length, from an empty string to a string consisting of
millions of characters.
Can contain any characters: A string can include letters (A-Z, a-z), digits (0-9), special characters
like @, #, $, %, etc., spaces, tabs, and even newlines.

  • Iterable: We can iterate through each character of a string using Python loops, like for, like for and while.

  • Support Slicing: We can slice a string to extract substrings with the help of [start : end] syntax.

  • Unicode Support: By default, strings in Python 3 are stored as Unicode. This allows us to handle international languages and emojis efficiently.

  • Dynamic Length: We can initialize a string of any length, from an empty string to a string consisting of millions of characters.

  • Can contain any characters: A string can include letters (A-Z, a-z), digits (0-9), special characters like @, #, $, %, etc., spaces, tabs, and even newlines.

Accessing Characters in String

0 1 2 3 4 5 6 C O D E A Z Y -7 -6 -5 -4 -3 -2 -1

C O D E A Z Y
-7 -6 -5 -4 -3 -2 -1
# python program to access characters in a string
# given string
s = "CODEAZY"
print("Given String:", s)
# accessing characters using indexing

Example:

  1. python program to access characters in a string

  2. given string

  3. s = "CODEAZY"

  4. print("Given String:", s) 5. # accessing characters using indexing 6. print("s[0] =", s[0]) 7. print("s[5] =", s[5])

Accessing String with Negative Indexing

Example:

python program to access characters from back of the string
# given string
s = "CODEAZY"
print("Given String:", s)
# accessing characters using negative indexing
print("s[-1] =", s[-1])
print("s[-6] =", s[-6])

  1. python program to access characters from back of the string

  4. print("Given String:", s)

String Slicing

The format for slicing a string is string_name[start : end]

python program to slice a string
# given string
s = “codeazy"
print("Given String:", s)
# getting characters from index 1 to 4
print("s[1:5] =", s[1:5])
print("s[:4] =", s[:4])
print("s[4:] =", s[4:])

Example:

  1. python program to slice a string

  7. print("s[:4] =", s[:4])

  8. print("s[4:] =", s[4:])

  9. print("s[::-1] =", s[::-1])

String Method

S.No String Method Description Program Output
1 upper() Converts string into
uppercase.		 text = "python"
print(text.upper())		 PYTHON
2 lower() Converts string into
lowercase.		 text = "PYTHON"
print(text.lower())		 python
3 capitalize() Capitalizes first letter text = "python programming"

programming
4 title() Capitalizes first letter of every
word.		 text = "python programming"
print(text.title())		 Python
Programming
5 swapcase() Changes uppercase to
lowercase and vice versa.		 text = "PyThOn"
print(text.swapcase())		 pYtHoN

String Method

6 casefold() Used for aggressive lowercase
comparison.		 text = "HELLO"
print(text.casefold())		 hello
7 strip() Removes spaces from both sides. text = "Python"
print(text.strip())		 Python
8 lstrip() Removes left spaces. text = "Python"
print(text.lstrip())		 Python
9 rstrip() Removes right spaces. text = "Python"
print(text.rstrip())		 Python
10 replace() Replaces old value with new
value.		 text = "I like Java"
print(text.replace("Java",
"Python"))		 I like Python

String Method

11 split() Converts string into list. text = "Python Java C"
print(text.split())		 ['Python', 'Java',
'C']
12 join() Join list elements into string.
join() is considered one of the most useful
Python string methods for combining
iterable strings.		 data = ['Python', 'Java',
'C']
print(" - ".join(data))		 Python - Java - C
13 find() Finds substring position.
Or we can say that it finds the first
occurrence of the specified value.		 text = "Python"
print(text.find("t"))		 2
If not found:
-1
14 index() Same as find() but gives error if not found. text = "Python"
print(text.index("h"))		 3
15 count() Counts occurrences. text = "Python
Programming"
print(text.count("m"))		 2

String Method

16 startswith() Checks starting word. text = "Python"

17 endswith() Checks ending word. text = "Python"
print(text.endswith("on"))		 True
18 isalpha() Checks alphabet only. text = "Python"
print(text.isalpha())		 True
19 isdigit() Checks digits only.
•
Use isdigit() when you only want standard
decimal digits, possibly in various scripts.
Great for parsing IDs or zip codes.		 text = "12345"
print(text.isdigit())		 True
20 isnumeric() Checks numeric values.
•
Use isnumeric() when you want to accept
numeric forms like fractions or superscripts.
Useful in math processing or display parsing.		 text = "123"
print(text.isnumeric())		 True

String Method

21 isalnum() Checks alphabets + numbers. text = "Python123"

22 islower() Checks lowercase. text = "python"
print(text.islower())		 True
23 isupper() Checks uppercase. text = "PYTHON"

25 center() Aligns string at center. text = "Python"

num = "25"

print(num.zfill(5))

title: Data Structures & Algorithms
description: Master DSA — arrays, linked lists, trees, graphs, sorting algorithms, and Big-O complexity.

subjects.title: Arrays & Complexity
subjects.notes:
Arrays are contiguous memory blocks. Big-O measures time/space efficiency.

```
O(1)  — constant    → array access by index
O(n)  — linear      → linear search
O(n²) — quadratic   → bubble sort
O(log n) — log      → binary search
```

```python
arr = [5, 2, 8, 1, 9]
# Linear search — O(n)
for i, val in enumerate(arr):
    if val == 8:
        print(f'Found at index {i}')
```

subjects.title: Linked Lists
Stacks & Queues
subjects.notes:
Stack: LIFO. Queue: FIFO.

```python
# Stack with list
stack = []
stack.append(1)  # push
stack.append(2)
stack.pop()      # 2 (LIFO)

# Queue with deque
from collections import deque
q = deque()
q.append('a')    # enqueue
q.append('b')
q.popleft()      # 'a' (FIFO)
```

subjects.title: Trees & BST
Sorting Algorithms
subjects.notes:
Common sorts:

| Algorithm | Best | Avg | Worst | Stable |
|-----------|------|-----|-------|--------|
| Bubble    | O(n) | O(n²)| O(n²)| Yes |
| Merge     | O(n log n)| O(n log n)| O(n log n)| Yes |
| Quick     | O(n log n)| O(n log n)| O(n²)| No |

```python
def merge_sort(arr):
    if len(arr) <= 1: return arr
    mid = len(arr) // 2
    L = merge_sort(arr[:mid])
    R = merge_sort(arr[mid:])
    return merge(L, R)
```

title: DevOps
description: Learn modern DevOps practices including Linux, Git, Docker, CI/CD, Kubernetes, Cloud, and Infrastructure Automation to build, deploy, and manage applications efficiently.

subjects.title: Linux Fundamentals
subjects.notes:
Linux is the foundation of most DevOps environments. Learn file navigation, permissions, users, processes, and essential commands.

```bash
pwd
ls -la
cd /var/www
mkdir project
cp file.txt backup.txt
mv backup.txt archive.txt
rm -rf temp
chmod 755 script.sh
chown ubuntu:ubuntu app
ps aux
top
systemctl status nginx
```

subjects.title: Git & GitHub
subjects.notes:
Git is a distributed version control system used to track code changes and collaborate with teams.

```bash
git init
git clone https://github.com/user/project.git
git status
git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit"
git branch feature-auth
git checkout feature-auth
git merge feature-auth
git push origin main
```

subjects.title: Docker Basics
subjects.notes:
Docker packages applications and dependencies into lightweight containers.

```dockerfile
FROM openjdk:21-jdk
WORKDIR /app
COPY target/app.jar app.jar
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["java","-jar","app.jar"]
```

```bash
docker build -t my-app .
docker images
docker run -d -p 8080:8080 my-app
docker ps
docker logs <container-id>
docker stop <container-id>
```

subjects.title: CI/CD with GitHub Actions
subjects.notes:
CI/CD automates building, testing, and deploying applications whenever code changes.

```yaml
name: Build

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-java@v4
        with:
          distribution: temurin
          java-version: '21'
      - run: ./mvnw clean package
```

subjects.title: Kubernetes Introduction
subjects.notes:
Kubernetes automates container deployment, scaling, and management across clusters.

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: spring-app
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: spring-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: spring-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: my-app:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
```

```bash
kubectl get pods
kubectl get services
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
kubectl delete deployment spring-app
```
